Debbie & Eric WOC Bound

Named to USOF 1995 World Orienteering Championship Team

Both Debbie Newell and Eric Bone ran well at the US Orienteering Federation Team Trials this June at French Creek. Debbie overcame a summer cold to post a 6th place overall finish and earn yet another spot on the US team, while Eric, named USOC "orienteer of the year" captured a combined 5th place and thus earned the opportunity to compete in a WOC for his first time. The big event takes place this August in Germany; both Orienteering North America and the Bearing will have follow up coverage. Meanwhile, we thought you might like to hear about Debbie and Eric's approach to the team trials, training, and the WOC; please see "The Debbie & Eric Interview" on page 2. -- rjh.

Great July Events in BC and Alberta

Sage Stomp and Canadian 5 Day

If you are heading to the Kamloops area for the first time this July, you've got a great orienteering experience awaiting you! If you've been there before, you know that Sage OC hosts top-notch events with quality maps in terrain that is nothing less than fantastic. OK, hope you pre-registered for this and the sure-to-be-fantastic Canadian 5 Day already. If not, there are still some very nice day-of-event registration options. Please see the schedule page or call the orienteering hotline for more details Thanks -- rjh.

Major Washington Summer Events

Super August and September Orienteering Near Home -- "A" Meet, Rogaine, and State Champs

Sure, park meets can be a lot of fun, but for some "real orienteering" there is nothing like an "A" level event on a new 1:15,000 scale map or a 12 or 24 hour rogaine on an enhanced USGS map regardless whether you are a seasoned competitive or recreational orienteer, or are just getting started in the sport. And thanks to the efforts of Sammamish, Ellensburg, Columbia River, and Eastern Washington O Cs, we will have three super events here in Washington this summer. (Cascade OC, after doing three "B" meets this spring and the "A" event last fall, is taking a breather, in no small part due to Nisqually's Carl Moore's kind offer to host the State Champs at Cle Elum this Sept.) All these events are detailed inside, along with the respective application. (Not only is it less expensive if you pre-register, if you don't, you may not be able to run the course you want nor get a pre-marked map. So please pay attention to the cut-off dates as you won't want to miss these great events.) -- rjh.

Page 2

The Debbie & Eric Interview

As asked by Rick Hood via email

"What were your expectations and game plan going into the team trials?"

Eric Bone -- After some satisfying runs at the Intercollegiates in April, I felt pretty confident that I would qualify for the WOC '95 team. Going into the longer race on Friday, I wanted to win because I felt that with a strong performance, I could and I hadn't topped a strong men's open field yet--I'd only won Red courses. I didn't really know what to expect in the short distance races on Saturday. I figured anywhere in the top six or eight was possible, depending on how I felt on race day. My main goal for the short races was to have fun; the article in O/NA made it sound like lots of fun.

Debbie -- In the months preceding the team Trials, I had confidence that I would make the WOC Team. My plan was to concentrate on physical conditioning on my own and use local meets and training events (by the way, thanks to Fred Veler, who organized several training events) to practice technical skills. Unfortunately, just before the Trials, I got the flu, so was not in peak physical condition. I didn't think I would have a chance at making the team without being completely healthy, so at that point I was just expecting to enjoy the trip.

"How did you feel about your performance; if you could change anything, what would it be?"

Eric -- Upon finishing the longer race on Friday, I was pretty disgusted with myself, because I'd made about 10 minutes of mistakes, but then I saw that I was in fifth place anyway. That made me feel somewhat better. My run showed me that I have what it takes physically, and that's going to be crucial in Germany. In the short distance morning race, I made a two and one half minute mistake, but otherwise had a pretty good run. Then in the afternoon, my technique was pretty crude and I settled into a mediocre place again. Part of me is disappointed that I didn't come away with a win or second in any of the trials races. I think I have the stuff but just couldn't put it together; the lack of big races this spring definitely hurt my race preparedness. All-in-all though, I'm very pleased that I made the team, and the COC second in the relays was just what the doctor ordered.

Debbie -- Considering the circumstances, I was fairly satisfied with my performance, except for the afternoon session of the Short Course Championships. I spaced out and ran from Control #9 to Control #11. By the time I went to #10 and returned to #11, I had lost 3-4 minutes, dropping me from 2nd or 3rd place (and a nearly automatic slot on the team) to 4th place (and the first alternate position).

"What are you doing in preparation for the big trip to WOC; what, if any goals have you set for yourself or the US team?"

Eric -- After a good, solid winter and spring of hard training, it's time for me to really scale back the workload, to just alternate races with rest in order to get into racing form. Between now and the WOC in August, most of the training I do will be technical and psychological preparation, so that I can just pick up a map and go full blast from the start almost automatically. For instance, I need to figure out exactly what mindset allowed me to perform better at the Intercollegiates than at the trials, then work on getting into that mode before big races.

I'm not really sure what is reasonable to expect from WOC this summer. One sure thing is that I'd like to break out of the bottom quarter or third, where U.S. competitors have typically placed. There are a few goals that are just kernels in the back of my mind right now. Only time will tell which of these grow so compelling that they become reality and which of them subside. I really think there is a possibility of good things from the relay team this year, but setting goals for others can lead to disappointment, so I'm trying not to invest too much emotional stake in that (but as much as I love relays, that will be difficult.)

Debbie -- To prepare for Germany, I am continuing the physical training program I have been following since last fall. I plan to get plenty of navigation training at the Sage Stomp in British Columbia and the International 5-day event in Alberta next month. As for goals at the WOC, mine is to make the final of whatever race I compete in. This year, all events except the relay have a qualification round, and there are no B and C finals as there were in 1993. And, although all five team members participate in the Short Course

Sidebar:
FYI -- Editor On Vacation
Peter Golde on short sabbatical

I talked Peter Golde, our Bearing 315 editor for the past few years, into taking a well deserved break for the July/August issue. (OK, actually I needed an orienteering favor and this was my "trading card".) Never fear though, Peter will be back at the helm for the September/October issue and with tales-a-plenty from his Colorado 7-day trip.

As always, the Bearing 315 staff (that is Peter) welcomes all your orienteering-related submissions. You may forward them to Peter via email at "petergo@microsoft.com" or by US Mail at 14422 NE 42nd Place #801, Bellevue, WA 98007. The deadline for the next issue is August 23rd.

In the meantime, I hope you enjoy this issue. I tried to solicit and include items that I thought you'd enjoy reading. -- Rick J. Hood

Page 3

Qualification, only 4 can run in the Classic and Relay. I will run in the Short Race and probably only one of the other two races. I can't set goals for the rest of the team, of course, but I hope everyone else is also looking to make the final. In the relay, if the women's team finished in the top 15, we would be ecstatic.

"It seems that competitive orienteering in the PNW has really come along a lot in the last few years, what is your impression and why?"

Eric -- I agree. I think orienteering in our area has begun to attract some new blood that is interested in really going for it. I think this is great, because not only does it mean that people like me who enjoy the thrill of trying to be the best that I can at the sport now have others to train and compete with, but it also means we are getting more folks who are keen on seeing O' thrive in the Northwest and that's good for everybody.

Debbie -- Yes, I think o' has gained in the past few years locally. The main reason, as I see it, is the impression local orienteers have made on the rest of the country. Once people like Eric Bone and the UW team began doing well nationally, the orienteering establishment noticed. That resulted in more publicity from the national o' magazine, which in turn resulted in renewed local interest in the sport. Regardless of whether one is competitive or not, competition is interesting to most people (think of how many people watch professional sports, but don't participate). So, having nationally-competitive orienteers in your region creates interest, which results in more awareness of the sport. And more people being aware of orienteering benefits all orienteers, not just the competitive ones.

"What advice would you give to the orienteers at the club level how to improve both physically and technically?"

Eric --

  • Seek out advice from some of the people in the area who are experienced in training and coaching--ask one of the staff or long-time club members at the next event to recommend someone to talk to; I know from experience that there are able people who are glad to help interested learners. (I put myself in the group of learners too. You just can't know too much in this sport!)
  • Volunteer to do course design or mapping for your club. It helps out your route-finding and map-reading skills, so you'll be doing both yourself and the rest of the orienteering community a favor.
  • Express your interest to the COC Training Coordinator (me) and ask him about the next series of low key, evening training events.
  • Start up an aerobic training program that fits within your schedule. Just 30 minutes, three times per week is much better than no regular exercise or only one or two sessions per week.
  • Look for others who share your interest in improving their skills and fitness. Training, or just talking periodically, with a group makes it even more fun.
  • Approach every meet with the intent to learn something from your experience or work on a particular technique (eg- pace counting or choosing an attack point.) Sometimes, of course, it's fun to just go out and enjoy the weather and let your mind wander, but even experienced people can learn from our local park events if they approach them with the right mindset.
  • Always remember that improvement only comes with training. If this weren't the case, it wouldn't be as much fun. Also, training is a process, not an event. Regular, smaller practice sessions are much more beneficial than an occasional big training camp.

Debbie --

  • My advice on how to improve physically and technically: find a way to motivate yourself. If you want to improve physically, take up running and/or cycling and do it on a regular basis. Unfortunately, you can't improve physically or technically without a lot of hard work. So, find a way to make yourself do that work. For me, it's self preservation. I like orienteering and competing, but I hate to train. I also have a low pain threshold. So, whatever I can do to avoid the potential pain associated with sore muscles and exhaustion, and still participate in orienteering meets, I'll do. Once you find your motivation, the rest is easy.
  • If you don't already, start running or cycling. If you don't like to run/cycle alone, join a club, or get together with friends.
  • Go to training events. The local o' clubs have held several training events so far this year.
  • Ask Eric Bone, COC's Training Coordinator, for suggestions.

More Washington Standouts

Paul Magnus & Nick Bone on National Team Too

Joining Debbie and Eric on the USOF "B" National Team is Paul Magnus, who took up orienteering, from a running background just three years ago! Additionally, Nick Bone is named to the National Development Team. (That's 4 COC members on the squad, for those who keep track of those kind of things. Though I view it as 4 nice folks from Western Washington, who are helping our sport grow by contributing time at local events through course setting, meet directing, sharing insights... -- rjh.) So, please join me in saying, "congratulations Paul and Nick."

Page 4

"Realizing that quick yet sound evaluative skills are a hallmark of world class orienteers, take the number of thumb compasses in Sweden, divide by the total number of O shoes in England and Germany, then subtract the number of scratches on Eric legs and arms after a typical meet times the percentage of times Debbie came down with poison oak if there was a plant within 50 km of the course. Now is the number positive or negative?"

Eric -- Neither--it's zero!

Debbie -- The number is negative. Considering the quantity of blood Eric leaves on blackberry bushes at a typical meet, you don't even have to calculate the rest.

Sidebar:

UW Takes 2nd at Intercollegiates

Bill Cusworth

"Despite strong efforts from the 4 team members, The Husky Orienteering team finished second to the US Military Academy (West Point) at the U.S. Intercollegiate Champs. The event took place on the Baileytown map in Harriman State Park in upstate N.Y. on April 22-23. Eric Bone became the male varsity champion for the 3rd year in a row after coming from behind on day 2. Nick Bone, Eric's brother, was still bothered by a nagging hip injury but managed to limp around the course to give decent times. Lisa Nordlund and Ellen Boomer put in good runs, especially on day 1, to keep the team close behind going into the 2nd day. Lisa and Ellen finished 5th and 7th among varsity women competitors. Bill Cusworth had good runs and would have finished 4th in the men's varsity competition but was too old (28) to qualify for college competition. Another notable achievement was a second place finish by Fred Schmitt, current WIOL (high school) champion, in the U.S. high school champs, held in conjunction with the college champs. A total of 13 people from the area flew to N.Y. to compete at the event. The times are listed in the results section.

From The Tales Of Sacajawea

-Elis Eberlein, SACO President

On May 20 we had our first event on a new map, "Mill Creek Project", in Walla Walla. We were lucky with the weather, and Rooks park is a great place for a meet with all the existing picnic facilities. We were all happy to see a lot of new faces willing to try out orienteering. Some of the performances by these "first-timers" were truly impressive. Frank and Holly really worked hard on trying to spread the word about the event, and they also got help from Don Davis, outdoor columnist at the Walla-Walla "Union-Bulletin". Don wrote a nice article in the newspaper a couple of weeks before the meet. Don, by the way is a runner, and I often met him and his Dalmatian when I was out mapping the area.

Now, about the mapping project. We got the base map from the US Army Corps of Engineers, who manage the area for flood control and recreation. They have the whole area surveyed down to 2' contours, but we decided 10' would be sufficient, and a scale of 1:5000 would be fine for the base map. The only disadvantage with the base map is that it did not have any vegetation features, but I am getting used to doing those "by hand" anyway. In traditional style, the time was running tight at the end to get the map finished. Frank and I were out making the last revisions only one week before the meet. We then also discovered that there is a minor disadvantage to have a map in an area managed by the corps. When the corps does something, they use bulldozers, and the map will need some updating! This Saturday was no exception, but they gave us a good description of the map revision, before it was actually finished.

To take advantage of the new map in Walla Walla, and to give our new members some more local events, we are planning for two events in Walla Walla this fall. I have just sent out the flyers with the schedule. So, please come to our events and bring some friends and help "spread the news" by distributing the flyer.

This leads to the another important message for all SACO members. Late this Summer and Fall, you will have plenty of opportunity to participate in several larger orienteering events right here in Eastern Washington. I encourage everyone to try to participate in these events. These meets are: 1. 2 day A-meet at Bullfrog Flats, Cle Elum, August 12-13; 2. Bald Mountain Rogaine, August 19-20; 3. Inland Northwest Cup, Spokane, September 16-17 ( I am the course setter on this one); and 4. Washington State Champs, Cle Elum Ridge, September 23-24.

Have a nice orienteering summer, and now I am off to Colorado to have just that!

"Make my Course"

Screenwriter & Orienteer Robert Miller's Thoughts

[At the end of May, Cascade OC held a planning retreat and Robert was kind enough to share his written "Random Thoughts, Ruminations and Brainstorms for the "O" Retreat" with us. They seem both timely and timeless to me, especially considering local park meets where relative newcomers are likely to experience orienteering; do you agree? -- rjh.]

"Course Setting: Too often I feel course design focuses on interesting control features instead of emphasizing the selection of interesting, i.e. navigationally challenging, legs: good legs offer multiple and equally attractive route choices. Good legs present complex navigation problems. The control points establish the leg and thus create the navigation problem, but in and of themselves are not as important as the course leg they create. In a well designed course, finding the control feature should not be the primary orienteering

Page 5

challenge. Naturally, when conditions permit, the course designer should strive to have both interesting legs and features, but when one must be sacrificed, the best orienteering is created with interesting legs and dull control features. Too often, course setters, especially beginners, select interesting features which are connected by uninteresting legs.

"Marker Hanging: A subtle variation of the above thought: My understanding is that orienteering is a land navigation sport, not an Easter egg hunt in the green for a blankety-blank marker. I suggest that every person who hangs a marker is required to chant the following mantra as they hang a marker: "LAND NAVIGATION, LAND NAVIGATION, LAND NAVIGATION." I suspect the temptation to hide markers is a consequence of poor course design. If a course leg does not offer a sufficiently challenging LAND NAVIGATION problem, the course setter is tempted to solve the problem by hiding the marker. Rather than think of orienteering as "looking for markers" one should think of the marker as the reward for solving the LAND NAVIGATION problem. Thus, with the leg successfully navigated, the marker should be visible, i.e. easy to find, i.e. easy to see, i.e. in plain view, i.e. not hidden in a dark green whatever."

"Nettle me this, O Man"

An excerpt from Cascade-Olympic Natural History

If you're interested in a colorful regional "trailside reference", consider Daniel Mathews' book (available in paperback around town). Now the reason I bring this up is that a few weeks ago Bill Cusworth, Eric Bone and I were working on an map/course project at a Salvation Army Camp (with programs designed for inner-city youth). We elected to trail blaze through a "greenish" area for the camp where there were nettles. And as fate would have it, we didn't manage to avoid them. Thus, my interest in the following excerpt from this most enjoyable book.

"Nettles are seen nowadays as something to avoid, but older traditions held them the most estimable of weeds. They're food: the young plants make delicious greens. They're fiber: the mature stem have been made into high-quality twine, cloth, and paper, substituting for flax in wartime as recently as World War II; and several tribes here made nettle twine nets for ducks and fish. They're medicine: the sting helped stoical Indian hunters stay awake through the night, and nettle tea has earned medicinal reputation worldwide.

"Nettle stingers are miniature hypodermic syringes, like bee and ant stingers, and employ the same irritant, formic acid. They evolved as a defense against browsing, but they don't save nettles from Milbert's tortoiseshell and other caterpillars. One folk remedy prescribes pulling up the nettle that bites you, to soothe the sting by crushing juice onto it from the nettle root. Wilting softens nettle stingers into harmlessness, and thorough steaming or drying renders them indetectable. If you ever camp in April or May near a bed of nettle roots a few inches high, cook them up. Wear long sleeves and gloves (or socks) over your hands, lop them with a knife, and steam them limp in half an inch of water. (Cooking them only al dente leaves enough bristle to worry your lips and tongue.) Butter, sour cream, feta or blue cheese, maybe crepes.."

OK, maybe when O suits give the protection of the batsuit I will be interested in being one with the nettles, until then I am glad we run in St. Edward in the Winter. -- rjh.

"Find the Word" O

A trim course or training aid of sorts; well maybe

The New York Times, is thinking about replacing their Sunday crossword puzzle with an orienteering-related "find the words" one but asked me to ask the Bearing 315 readers to try out this sample. (The puzzle follow the results section.) Actually, the editor said, we should just keep trying it out until he "gets back to me".

OK: Here are the course options:

  • Short course: find any 6 of the words from the left section that corresponds to a clue from the list on the right
  • Intermediate course: find any 13 of the words....
  • Advanced course: find all of the 20 words....

Timing: Self-timed; call in your times to Rick at 367-2606, if you like; current advanced course record is Linda's 17:40, though I didn't give her the hint that all the missing words are listed alphabetically in the clue list and pertain to orienteering activities during August and September here in Washington.

Bonus Option: If you do this while running a trail, deduct 20 seconds per word found and circled. (If you are contemplating doing it while driving, please call me first, so I can stay off the road.) -- rjh.

Just for the fun of it!

Bullfrog Flats A-Meet Director John Sincock's Thoughts

"I'm excited about this new orienteering event in August. It's a new map, an area not previously used for orienteering. This is always exciting in itself, what with courses set over terrain that isn't already familiar. This also leads itself to interesting insights when comparing notes with others after completing a course.

"I want to emphasize that this meet is for everyone to enjoy. Don't feel intimidated by names like "championship" or "sanctioned A-Meet". These events are important, but this meet, like all "O" meets, has courses for every level of skill. Beginners are especially encouraged to attend. Orienteering meets are social occasions, and nothing help improve skills better than comparing notes with other orienteers as to route choices or disastrous mistakes.

"So, make plans to visit Cle Elum this August. Meet visiting orienteers from other parts of the continent. Explore the woods. Sightsee in Roslyn and nearby Lake Easton. But most of all, be prepared to have fun!"

Sidebar:

Bald Mt. Rogaine Field Report

by Terradan Landchild, Event Director

Several of us visited the Rogaine event area last weekend, 24-25 June to field-check the proposed mapped control locations. We were all very impressed with the area! The terrain includes many interesting topographic features and some outstanding scenery! I was impressed at how well the USGS contour base accurately described the topography. Even as only an intermediate orienteer, my navigation was usually dead on target.

The terrain is also very runnable, generally open pine mixed forest. Some areas do have some deadfall, but most of the area is fast! Although well roaded, ample opportunity exists for forested and wild route choices. Fast runners may take more advantage of the roads.

We also found game trails everywhere for quick hiking. Not surprising considering the abundant wildlife we found. The place is teeming with deer and elk (we were awakened Sunday morning by very nearby elk 'bugling'). I believe everyone saw deer and elk!

I would strongly recommend recreational Rogainers to focus on the many incredible viewpoint control locations! What can I say, words fail me. Simply an outstanding area!!! Hope to see you there!

If you still need additional info and a registration form, send me your mailing address and I'll get it right out! Please hurry, registration deadline is August 1st, after that there is a late fee!

ROGAINE Performance Planning

by Bob Reddick

For both orienteering and rogaining, it's very helpful to know your personal travel rates and pace counts under various conditions and in different terrain. Her are a few tried and tested ideas which you might use to prepare you and your team for the next rogaine you enter, and for those long legs on orienteering courses:

GET YOUR PACE COUNT FOR 100 METERS:

Suggested method -- walk around a high school 1/4 mile track. That's 1320 feet or 403 meters. Count your paces (that's two steps), and figure how many paces for 100 meters level. Do it again at an easy jogging pace. Then go get actual pace counts in a local park with an orienteering map. Pick both uphill and downhill legs to try, and see what variances you get. If you and your team intend to run some legs, such as on logging roads and easy trails, get your pace counts for running as well. This whole experiment should take you less than two hours. Reduce these numbers to a

Page 7

simple chart to memorize and also stick to your watchband or the back of your compass. Like the following example (but find your own parameters):

100 Meters Walk Run
Pace Time
UP/NIGHT 75 2:00
DOWN: 65 1:10
LEVEL: 60 1:00

To use the above, a simple example is a leg of 500 meters along a level trail, where you then want to leave it. So plan on walking on the level for 5 x 1:00 = 5 minutes, and pacing out (60 paces/100 m.) x 5 = 300 paces for the distance. With your partner doing the same, you should agree on where to turn off.

Now that you have some orienteering-type data, it's time to plan some long-distance performance rates, based on a real-life performance:

24-hour pace for an elite team (Gagarin-Pilon, Table Mt. 1993)
UP/NIGHT: 2.4 KPH or 100 m. in 2:30 min.
DOWN speed: 3.6 KPH or 100 m. in 1:40 min.
LEVEL speed: 5.3 KPH or 100 m. in 1:08 min. (bursts to 9 KPH)
OVERALL: 3.63 KPH or 100 m. in 1:39 min.

24-hour pace for my team (develop your own realistic ones):
UP/NIGHT: 1.6 KPH or 100 m. in 3:45 min.
DOWN speed: 2.4 KPH or 100 m. in 2:30 min.
LEVEL speed: 3.5 KPH or 100 m. in 1:43 min. (no jogging planned)
OVERALL: 2.42 KPH or 100 m. in 2:29 min.

Another neat trick is to make a pace and time scale for your overall rate on a thin strip of paper, and glue it to your compass edge to measure with. Make one up for the scale of map to be used for the rogaine, if known. For Bald Mountain Rogaine coming up on August 19-20, the scale will probably be 1:36,000. Your paper scale might look like this:

0 5 10 15 20 25 30 min.
0 120 240 360 480 600 720 paces
0 200 400 600 800 1000 1200 meters
0 0.5 1.0 1.5 2.0 2.5 3.0 3.33 cm. (use this to size your bar)

HERE'S ANOTHER WAY TO FIND YOUR TEAM'S OVERALL RATE IN JUST 30 MINUTES:

  1. Set downhill, off-trail but not real steep (no sliding or tree-grabbing) course for 1 km.
  2. Walk this course with your partner. This should be a leg never taken before on some topographic map.
  3. Take times and calculate distance actually traveled by measuring map.
  4. Calculate rate in kilometers/hour (KPH).
  5. Use this downhill rate for your best overall rate on a rogaine course.

If you've done a rogaine before, see if this rate matches your actual performance in that event. Make up the table as shown above using 66% of your overall rate for the UP/NIGHT legs, and 146% of that rate for the LEVEL rate.

WATER AND ENDURANCE FACTOR:
Research shows that your stomach can empty about 800 ml. or 26.4 oz. of water per hour. This varies a little by body weight -- these figures are for a 150-pound person. Your body needs and continually consumes water at about that rate while rogaining. You must find a way to ensure that your team will keep this critical fuel in your system. What do you suppose will happen if you keep moving but don't drink for as long as two hours? Only one hour? And what will happen if SOMEONE on your team fails to drink on schedule?

Actual results for other non-drinkers have been: Leg cramps, severe headaches, fainting, severe dehydration, early retirement, a Search and Rescue call, and a hospital trip. These have all happened in past O' meets and rogaines. You had better set up a drinking schedule and find a way to stick to it. Remember that the feeling of thirst is an emergency signal that comes much too late to be of help. THINK TO DRINK - YOU MUST DRINK TO THINK!

Good luck in your future long-distance navigation events!

Sidebar:

Field Protection that Works

From Bugs and Sun

I know the title might be the opening line of a Harvey Friedman story, but actually I just wanted to share with you two relatively new products that really work in an orienteering setting. (Info comes from Dr. Forgey, who wrote Wilderness Medicine and spoke at REI recently.)

"DEET Plus" to keep those bugs away. It is as effective as 100% DEET, but with only 17% DEET, bound with a polymer to enhance the effectiveness, the skin absorption issue is much reduced. (About 15% of DEET applied is absorbed through the skin; some have noticed sensitivity.) DEET Plus also contains a 2% Black Fly repellent, which is needed in products where the DEET level is less than 30%. The product sell for under $4 at Sportsmart locally. (I've used it and like it.)

"CopperTone Sport Ultra Sweatproof" is a breathable waterproof suntan lotion. The word "Sport" in the title is key for this Gortex-like protection process. You don't sweat it off, but, unlike with Bullfrog lotion, your skin can breathe, thus you don't blow micro-holes through your protective layer. I've got Linda using it; her only negative comment that it takes a good scrubbing with a loofah to get it off--what more could an orienteer want. You can get an 8 oz bottle at Safeway for about $8.

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and the new Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need?
The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for rent at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair of socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Bald Mountain Rogaine
the North American Rogaine Championships
August 19-20, 1995

Very brief explanation: Using a topographic map, teams (generally pairs) strive to reach as many mapped control points as possible with a 12 or 24 time limit.

Location: The site lies on the east slope of the Cascade Mountains, just east of the of the William O Douglas Wilderness Area, about 30 miles east of the summit of Mt. Rainier on Hwy 410. Allow three hours to reach the site from Seattle. (Detailed driving instruction and motel/camping options mailed with entry form acknowledgment.)

Other 'O-Vents': The Rogaine is the grand finale of a fantastic nine-day orienteering offering by SAMM, CROC, and EOC. Call the hotline at (206) 783-3866 and then select option 4 for more details.

Participation Logistics: Your team will receive a USGS-type topographic map showing the location of all the controls when you check in beginning at about 9 am Saturday. You will be asked to plan your most likely route and leave a copy at registration. A mass start occurs at noon. Depending on the option you choose, you will have 12 or 24 hours to reach as many of the controls as you can. (The more distant and difficult control locations will be worth more points and have a higher control ID to indicate such.) As each control flag you must punch your control card and also sign an "intention sheet" as to where you plan to go next. Regardless, make sure to return to the base camp within your time limit, severe penalties are given for returning late.

Food: By about 6 pm, food service will be set up and available at the "Hash House" located at base camp. The smart teams loop back to rest, refresh, and stoke up for the remainder of the event. Food service continues until the event ends with an Award Ceremony at 1 pm on Sunday. Our backcountry chef, Kent Verbeck, has outlined a sumptuous feast, including fresh Yakima Valley fruits, pastas, and a barbecue!

More Details: Highly competitive teams may reach all the controls. But those out to enjoy some of the great scenery and some challenging navigation will "win" no matter how many controls they find. Many teams volunteer to do a one-control pick-up after the events--this add to the fun since you might drive or hike to a portion of the course you didn't visit before.

Registration: Pre-registration is required. The deadline is August 1st, unless the 50 team maximum is reached earlier, so register early! Send you entry form to: CROC c/o Landchild, 3802 NE 112nd Ave, Vancouver, WA 98682. Fees are $60 per two-person team, and $30 for each additional team member. (Lots of food and other support services, user fees, and organizational costs are covered by the team registration fee.)

Course Options: Your team may register for either the 24 hour "classic" or the 12 hour "recreational" course option. The former will run from noon Saturday to noon Sunday. The latter can be any 12 hours within the 24 hour noon Saturday to noon Sunday period--your team can "clock out" and "in" at the Hash House as often as you like.

Registration Categories: On each course of the two courses, your team may register in any one of the following seven categories: Men, Women, Mixed, and Masters( where all members, whether Men, Women, or Mixed, are over 40) or open-group (any team with more than two participants). The organizer reserves the right to combine categories.

What to Bring?: A compatible partner is most important. Teams are required to stay together (within voice distance). You also must have an ability to navigate with a map and compass. You must carry a whistle, compass, watch, day pack with emergency gear, water (at least two canteens per person), trail food, sunglasses, sunblock, flashlight, trowel and tp... Do not bring other maps nor aerial photographs to use on the course, nor altimeters, GPS devices, etc. (All travel while on the course must be by foot.) We recommend that you leave your pets at home. Please note that our base camp, while nice for camping, does not have water (i.e., it is a dry camp). Thus, everyone should bring their own water for the weekend. Lastly, you are welcome to pitch your tent at base camp anytime after noon on Friday, but please remember that food service doesn't start until Saturday afternoon.

Practice Course: A "trim" or practice course is located near White Pass on Hwy 12. Send $5 for one or $8 for two maps to the above listed registration address. (This area was recently featured in Orienteering North America articles.)

Sounds like fun! I look forward to seeing you there!

Terradan Landchild
Bald Mountain Rogaine Event Director

Named to USOF 1995 World Orienteering Championship Team

Both Debbie Newell and Eric Bone ran well at the US Orienteering Federation Team Trials this June at French Creek. Debbie overcame a summer cold to post a 6th place overall finish and earn yet another spot on the US team, while Eric, named USOC "orienteer of the year" captured a combined 5th place and thus earned the opportunity to compete in a WOC for his first time. The big event takes place this August in Germany; both Orienteering North America and the Bearing will have follow up coverage. Meanwhile, we thought you might like to hear about Debbie and Eric's approach to the team trials, training, and the WOC; please see "The Debbie & Eric Interview" on page 2. -- rjh.
Great July Events in BC and Alberta

If you are heading to the Kamloops area for the first time this July, you've got a great orienteering experience awaiting you! If you've been there before, you know that Sage OC hosts top-notch events with quality maps in terrain that is nothing less than fantastic. OK, hope you pre-registered for this and the sure-to-be-fantastic Canadian 5 Day already. If not, there are still some very nice day-of-event registration options. Please see the schedule page or call the orienteering hotline for more details Thanks -- rjh.
Super August and September Orienteering Near Home -- "A" Meet, Rogaine, and State Champs

Sure, park meets can be a lot of fun, but for some "real orienteering" there is nothing like an "A" level event on a new 1:15,000 scale map or a 12 or 24 hour rogaine on an enhanced USGS map regardless whether you are a seasoned competitive or recreational orienteer, or are just getting started in the sport. And thanks to the efforts of Sammamish, Ellensburg, Columbia River, and Eastern Washington O Cs, we will have three super events here in Washington this summer. (Cascade OC, after doing three "B" meets this spring and the "A" event last fall, is taking a breather, in no small part due to Nisqually's Carl Moore's kind offer to host the State Champs at Cle Elum this Sept.) All these events are detailed inside, along with the respective application. (Not only is it less expensive if you pre-register, if you don't, you may not be able to run the course you want nor get a pre-marked map. So please pay attention to the cut-off dates as you won't want to miss these great events.) -- rjh.
As asked by Rick Hood via email
"What were your expectations and game plan going into the team trials?"

Eric Bone -- After some satisfying runs at the Intercollegiates in April, I felt pretty confident that I would qualify for the WOC '95 team. Going into the longer race on Friday, I wanted to win because I felt that with a strong performance, I could and I hadn't topped a strong men's open field yet--I'd only won Red courses. I didn't really know what to expect in the short distance races on Saturday. I figured anywhere in the top six or eight was possible, depending on how I felt on race day. My main goal for the short races was to have fun; the article in O/NA made it sound like lots of fun.

Debbie -- In the months preceding the team Trials, I had confidence that I would make the WOC Team. My plan was to concentrate on physical conditioning on my own and use local meets and training events (by the way, thanks to Fred Veler, who organized several training events) to practice technical skills. Unfortunately, just before the Trials, I got the flu, so was not in peak physical condition. I didn't think I would have a chance at making the team without being completely healthy, so at that point I was just expecting to enjoy the trip.
"How did you feel about your performance; if you could change anything, what would it be?"

Eric -- Upon finishing the longer race on Friday, I was pretty disgusted with myself, because I'd made about 10 minutes of mistakes, but then I saw that I was in fifth place anyway. That made me feel somewhat better. My run showed me that I have what it takes physically, and that's going to be crucial in Germany. In the short distance morning race, I made a two and one half minute mistake, but otherwise had a pretty good run. Then in the afternoon, my technique was pretty crude and I settled into a mediocre place again. Part of me is disappointed that I didn't come away with a win or second in any of the trials races. I think I have the stuff but just couldn't put it together; the lack of big races this spring definitely hurt my race preparedness. All-in-all though, I'm very pleased that I made the team, and the COC second in the relays was just what the doctor ordered.

Debbie -- Considering the circumstances, I was fairly satisfied with my performance, except for the afternoon session of the Short Course Championships. I spaced out and ran from Control #9 to Control #11. By the time I went to #10 and returned to #11, I had lost 3-4 minutes, dropping me from 2nd or 3rd place (and a nearly automatic slot on the team) to 4th place (and the first alternate position).
"What are you doing in preparation for the big trip to WOC; what, if any goals have you set for yourself or the US team?"

Eric -- After a good, solid winter and spring of hard training, it's time for me to really scale back the workload, to just alternate races with rest in order to get into racing form. Between now and the WOC in August, most of the training I do will be technical and psychological preparation, so that I can just pick up a map and go full blast from the start almost automatically. For instance, I need to figure out exactly what mindset allowed me to perform better at the Intercollegiates than at the trials, then work on getting into that mode before big races.

I'm not really sure what is reasonable to expect from WOC this summer. One sure thing is that I'd like to break out of the bottom quarter or third, where U.S. competitors have typically placed. There are a few goals that are just kernels in the back of my mind right now. Only time will tell which of these grow so compelling that they become reality and which of them subside. I really think there is a possibility of good things from the relay team this year, but setting goals for others can lead to disappointment, so I'm trying not to invest too much emotional stake in that (but as much as I love relays, that will be difficult.)

Debbie -- To prepare for Germany, I am continuing the physical training program I have been following since last fall. I plan to get plenty of navigation training at the Sage Stomp in British Columbia and the International 5-day event in Alberta next month. As for goals at the WOC, mine is to make the final of whatever race I compete in. This year, all events except the relay have a qualification round, and there are no B and C finals as there were in 1993. And, although all five team members participate in the Short Course
Sidebar: FYI -- Editor On Vacation Peter Golde on short sabbatical

I talked Peter Golde, our Bearing 315 editor for the past few years, into taking a well deserved break for the July/August issue. (OK, actually I needed an orienteering favor and this was my "trading card".) Never fear though, Peter will be back at the helm for the September/October issue and with tales-a-plenty from his Colorado 7-day trip.

As always, the Bearing 315 staff (that is Peter) welcomes all your orienteering-related submissions. You may forward them to Peter via email at "petergo@microsoft.com" or by US Mail at 14422 NE 42nd Place #801, Bellevue, WA 98007. The deadline for the next issue is August 23rd.
In the meantime, I hope you enjoy this issue. I tried to solicit and include items that I thought you'd enjoy reading. -- Rick J. Hood

Qualification, only 4 can run in the Classic and Relay. I will run in the Short Race and probably only one of the other two races. I can't set goals for the rest of the team, of course, but I hope everyone else is also looking to make the final. In the relay, if the women's team finished in the top 15, we would be ecstatic.
"It seems that competitive orienteering in the PNW has really come along a lot in the last few years, what is your impression and why?"

Eric -- I agree. I think orienteering in our area has begun to attract some new blood that is interested in really going for it. I think this is great, because not only does it mean that people like me who enjoy the thrill of trying to be the best that I can at the sport now have others to train and compete with, but it also means we are getting more folks who are keen on seeing O' thrive in the Northwest and that's good for everybody.

Debbie -- Yes, I think o' has gained in the past few years locally. The main reason, as I see it, is the impression local orienteers have made on the rest of the country. Once people like Eric Bone and the UW team began doing well nationally, the orienteering establishment noticed. That resulted in more publicity from the national o' magazine, which in turn resulted in renewed local interest in the sport. Regardless of whether one is competitive or not, competition is interesting to most people (think of how many people watch professional sports, but don't participate). So, having nationally-competitive orienteers in your region creates interest, which results in more awareness of the sport. And more people being aware of orienteering benefits all orienteers, not just the competitive ones.
"What advice would you give to the orienteers at the club level how to improve both physically and technically?"

Seek out advice from some of the people in the area who are experienced in training and coaching--ask one of the staff or long-time club members at the next event to recommend someone to talk to; I know from experience that there are able people who are glad to help interested learners. (I put myself in the group of learners too. You just can't know too much in this sport!)
Volunteer to do course design or mapping for your club. It helps out your route-finding and map-reading skills, so you'll be doing both yourself and the rest of the orienteering community a favor.
Express your interest to the COC Training Coordinator (me) and ask him about the next series of low key, evening training events.
Start up an aerobic training program that fits within your schedule. Just 30 minutes, three times per week is much better than no regular exercise or only one or two sessions per week.
Look for others who share your interest in improving their skills and fitness. Training, or just talking periodically, with a group makes it even more fun.

Approach every meet with the intent to learn something from your experience or work on a particular technique (eg- pace counting or choosing an attack point.) Sometimes, of course, it's fun to just go out and enjoy the weather and let your mind wander, but even experienced people can learn from our local park events if they approach them with the right mindset.

Always remember that improvement only comes with training. If this weren't the case, it wouldn't be as much fun. Also, training is a process, not an event. Regular, smaller practice sessions are much more beneficial than an occasional big training camp.

My advice on how to improve physically and technically: find a way to motivate yourself. If you want to improve physically, take up running and/or cycling and do it on a regular basis. Unfortunately, you can't improve physically or technically without a lot of hard work. So, find a way to make yourself do that work. For me, it's self preservation. I like orienteering and competing, but I hate to train. I also have a low pain threshold. So, whatever I can do to avoid the potential pain associated with sore muscles and exhaustion, and still participate in orienteering meets, I'll do. Once you find your motivation, the rest is easy.
If you don't already, start running or cycling. If you don't like to run/cycle alone, join a club, or get together with friends.
Go to training events. The local o' clubs have held several training events so far this year.
Ask Eric Bone, COC's Training Coordinator, for suggestions.
Paul Magnus & Nick Bone on National Team Too

Joining Debbie and Eric on the USOF "B" National Team is Paul Magnus, who took up orienteering, from a running background just three years ago! Additionally, Nick Bone is named to the National Development Team. (That's 4 COC members on the squad, for those who keep track of those kind of things. Though I view it as 4 nice folks from Western Washington, who are helping our sport grow by contributing time at local events through course setting, meet directing, sharing insights... -- rjh.) So, please join me in saying, "congratulations Paul and Nick."

"Realizing that quick yet sound evaluative skills are a hallmark of world class orienteers, take the number of thumb compasses in Sweden, divide by the total number of O shoes in England and Germany, then subtract the number of scratches on Eric legs and arms after a typical meet times the percentage of times Debbie came down with poison oak if there was a plant within 50 km of the course. Now is the number positive or negative?"
Debbie -- The number is negative. Considering the quantity of blood Eric leaves on blackberry bushes at a typical meet, you don't even have to calculate the rest.

"Despite strong efforts from the 4 team members, The Husky Orienteering team finished second to the US Military Academy (West Point) at the U.S. Intercollegiate Champs. The event took place on the Baileytown map in Harriman State Park in upstate N.Y. on April 22-23. Eric Bone became the male varsity champion for the 3rd year in a row after coming from behind on day 2. Nick Bone, Eric's brother, was still bothered by a nagging hip injury but managed to limp around the course to give decent times. Lisa Nordlund and Ellen Boomer put in good runs, especially on day 1, to keep the team close behind going into the 2nd day. Lisa and Ellen finished 5th and 7th among varsity women competitors. Bill Cusworth had good runs and would have finished 4th in the men's varsity competition but was too old (28) to qualify for college competition. Another notable achievement was a second place finish by Fred Schmitt, current WIOL (high school) champion, in the U.S. high school champs, held in conjunction with the college champs. A total of 13 people from the area flew to N.Y. to compete at the event. The times are listed in the results section.

On May 20 we had our first event on a new map, "Mill Creek Project", in Walla Walla. We were lucky with the weather, and Rooks park is a great place for a meet with all the existing picnic facilities. We were all happy to see a lot of new faces willing to try out orienteering. Some of the performances by these "first-timers" were truly impressive. Frank and Holly really worked hard on trying to spread the word about the event, and they also got help from Don Davis, outdoor columnist at the Walla-Walla "Union-Bulletin". Don wrote a nice article in the newspaper a couple of weeks before the meet. Don, by the way is a runner, and I often met him and his Dalmatian when I was out mapping the area.

Now, about the mapping project. We got the base map from the US Army Corps of Engineers, who manage the area for flood control and recreation. They have the whole area surveyed down to 2' contours, but we decided 10' would be sufficient, and a scale of 1:5000 would be fine for the base map. The only disadvantage with the base map is that it did not have any vegetation features, but I am getting used to doing those "by hand" anyway. In traditional style, the time was running tight at the end to get the map finished. Frank and I were out making the last revisions only one week before the meet. We then also discovered that there is a minor disadvantage to have a map in an area managed by the corps. When the corps does something, they use bulldozers, and the map will need some updating! This Saturday was no exception, but they gave us a good description of the map revision, before it was actually finished.

To take advantage of the new map in Walla Walla, and to give our new members some more local events, we are planning for two events in Walla Walla this fall. I have just sent out the flyers with the schedule. So, please come to our events and bring some friends and help "spread the news" by distributing the flyer.

This leads to the another important message for all SACO members. Late this Summer and Fall, you will have plenty of opportunity to participate in several larger orienteering events right here in Eastern Washington. I encourage everyone to try to participate in these events. These meets are: 1. 2 day A-meet at Bullfrog Flats, Cle Elum, August 12-13; 2. Bald Mountain Rogaine, August 19-20; 3. Inland Northwest Cup, Spokane, September 16-17 ( I am the course setter on this one); and 4. Washington State Champs, Cle Elum Ridge, September 23-24.
Have a nice orienteering summer, and now I am off to Colorado to have just that!

[At the end of May, Cascade OC held a planning retreat and Robert was kind enough to share his written "Random Thoughts, Ruminations and Brainstorms for the "O" Retreat" with us. They seem both timely and timeless to me, especially considering local park meets where relative newcomers are likely to experience orienteering; do you agree? -- rjh.]

"Course Setting: Too often I feel course design focuses on interesting control features instead of emphasizing the selection of interesting, i.e. navigationally challenging, legs: good legs offer multiple and equally attractive route choices. Good legs present complex navigation problems. The control points establish the leg and thus create the navigation problem, but in and of themselves are not as important as the course leg they create. In a well designed course, finding the control feature should not be the primary orienteering

challenge. Naturally, when conditions permit, the course designer should strive to have both interesting legs and features, but when one must be sacrificed, the best orienteering is created with interesting legs and dull control features. Too often, course setters, especially beginners, select interesting features which are connected by uninteresting legs.

"Marker Hanging: A subtle variation of the above thought: My understanding is that orienteering is a land navigation sport, not an Easter egg hunt in the green for a blankety-blank marker. I suggest that every person who hangs a marker is required to chant the following mantra as they hang a marker: "LAND NAVIGATION, LAND NAVIGATION, LAND NAVIGATION." I suspect the temptation to hide markers is a consequence of poor course design. If a course leg does not offer a sufficiently challenging LAND NAVIGATION problem, the course setter is tempted to solve the problem by hiding the marker. Rather than think of orienteering as "looking for markers" one should think of the marker as the reward for solving the LAND NAVIGATION problem. Thus, with the leg successfully navigated, the marker should be visible, i.e. easy to find, i.e. easy to see, i.e. in plain view, i.e. not hidden in a dark green whatever."
An excerpt from Cascade-Olympic Natural History

If you're interested in a colorful regional "trailside reference", consider Daniel Mathews' book (available in paperback around town). Now the reason I bring this up is that a few weeks ago Bill Cusworth, Eric Bone and I were working on an map/course project at a Salvation Army Camp (with programs designed for inner-city youth). We elected to trail blaze through a "greenish" area for the camp where there were nettles. And as fate would have it, we didn't manage to avoid them. Thus, my interest in the following excerpt from this most enjoyable book.

"Nettles are seen nowadays as something to avoid, but older traditions held them the most estimable of weeds. They're food: the young plants make delicious greens. They're fiber: the mature stem have been made into high-quality twine, cloth, and paper, substituting for flax in wartime as recently as World War II; and several tribes here made nettle twine nets for ducks and fish. They're medicine: the sting helped stoical Indian hunters stay awake through the night, and nettle tea has earned medicinal reputation worldwide.

"Nettle stingers are miniature hypodermic syringes, like bee and ant stingers, and employ the same irritant, formic acid. They evolved as a defense against browsing, but they don't save nettles from Milbert's tortoiseshell and other caterpillars. One folk remedy prescribes pulling up the nettle that bites you, to soothe the sting by crushing juice onto it from the nettle root. Wilting softens nettle stingers into harmlessness, and thorough steaming or drying renders them indetectable. If you ever camp in April or May near a bed of nettle roots a few inches high, cook them up. Wear long sleeves and gloves (or socks) over your hands, lop them with a knife, and steam them limp in half an inch of water. (Cooking them only al dente leaves enough bristle to worry your lips and tongue.) Butter, sour cream, feta or blue cheese, maybe crepes.."
OK, maybe when O suits give the protection of the batsuit I will be interested in being one with the nettles, until then I am glad we run in St. Edward in the Winter. -- rjh.

The last year has been a busy and exciting one with the birth of my first child. And unfortunately I have not been able to attend many events. A trim course or training aid of sorts; well maybe

The New York Times, is thinking about replacing their Sunday crossword puzzle with an orienteering-related "find the words" one but asked me to ask the Bearing 315 readers to try out this sample. (The puzzle follow the results section.) Actually, the editor said, we should just keep trying it out until he "gets back to me".
OK: Here are the course options:

Intermediate course: find any 13 of the words....

Just for the fun of it!
Bullfrog Flats A-Meet Director John Sincock's Thoughts

"I'm excited about this new orienteering event in August. It's a new map, an area not previously used for orienteering. This is always exciting in itself, what with courses set over terrain that isn't already familiar. This also leads itself to interesting insights when comparing notes with others after completing a course.

"I want to emphasize that this meet is for everyone to enjoy. Don't feel intimidated by names like "championship" or "sanctioned A-Meet". These events are important, but this meet, like all "O" meets, has courses for every level of skill. Beginners are especially encouraged to attend. Orienteering meets are social occasions, and nothing help improve skills better than comparing notes with other orienteers as to route choices or disastrous mistakes.

"So, make plans to visit Cle Elum this August. Meet visiting orienteers from other parts of the continent. Explore the woods. Sightsee in Roslyn and nearby Lake Easton. But most of all, be prepared to have fun!"

Several of us visited the Rogaine event area last weekend, 24-25 June to field-check the proposed mapped control locations. We were all very impressed with the area! The terrain includes many interesting topographic features and some outstanding scenery! I was impressed at how well the USGS contour base accurately described the topography. Even as only an intermediate orienteer, my navigation was usually dead on target.

The terrain is also very runnable, generally open pine mixed forest. Some areas do have some deadfall, but most of the area is fast! Although well roaded, ample opportunity exists for forested and wild route choices. Fast runners may take more advantage of the roads.

We also found game trails everywhere for quick hiking. Not surprising considering the abundant wildlife we found. The place is teeming with deer and elk (we were awakened Sunday morning by very nearby elk 'bugling'). I believe everyone saw deer and elk!
I would strongly recommend recreational Rogainers to focus on the many incredible viewpoint control locations! What can I say, words fail me. Simply an outstanding area!!! Hope to see you there!
If you still need additional info and a registration form, send me your mailing address and I'll get it right out! Please hurry, registration deadline is August 1st, after that there is a late fee!

For both orienteering and rogaining, it's very helpful to know your personal travel rates and pace counts under various conditions and in different terrain. Her are a few tried and tested ideas which you might use to prepare you and your team for the next rogaine you enter, and for those long legs on orienteering courses:
GET YOUR PACE COUNT FOR 100 METERS:

Suggested method -- walk around a high school 1/4 mile track. That's 1320 feet or 403 meters. Count your paces (that's two steps), and figure how many paces for 100 meters level. Do it again at an easy jogging pace. Then go get actual pace counts in a local park with an orienteering map. Pick both uphill and downhill legs to try, and see what variances you get. If you and your team intend to run some legs, such as on logging roads and easy trails, get your pace counts for running as well. This whole experiment should take you less than two hours. Reduce these numbers to a
simple chart to memorize and also stick to your watchband or the back of your compass. Like the following example (but find your own parameters):

To use the above, a simple example is a leg of 500 meters along a level trail, where you then want to leave it. So plan on walking on the level for 5 x 1:00 = 5 minutes, and pacing out (60 paces/100 m.) x 5 = 300 paces for the distance. With your partner doing the same, you should agree on where to turn off.
Now that you have some orienteering-type data, it's time to plan some long-distance performance rates, based on a real-life performance:

24-hour pace for an elite team (Gagarin-Pilon, Table Mt. 1993) UP/NIGHT: 2.4 KPH or 100 m. in 2:30 min. DOWN speed: 3.6 KPH or 100 m. in 1:40 min. LEVEL speed: 5.3 KPH or 100 m. in 1:08 min. (bursts to 9 KPH) OVERALL: 3.63 KPH or 100 m. in 1:39 min.

24-hour pace for my team (develop your own realistic ones): UP/NIGHT: 1.6 KPH or 100 m. in 3:45 min. DOWN speed: 2.4 KPH or 100 m. in 2:30 min. LEVEL speed: 3.5 KPH or 100 m. in 1:43 min. (no jogging planned) OVERALL: 2.42 KPH or 100 m. in 2:29 min.

Another neat trick is to make a pace and time scale for your overall rate on a thin strip of paper, and glue it to your compass edge to measure with. Make one up for the scale of map to be used for the rogaine, if known. For Bald Mountain Rogaine coming up on August 19-20, the scale will probably be 1:36,000. Your paper scale might look like this:
HERE'S ANOTHER WAY TO FIND YOUR TEAM'S OVERALL RATE IN JUST 30 MINUTES:
Set downhill, off-trail but not real steep (no sliding or tree-grabbing) course for 1 km.
Walk this course with your partner. This should be a leg never taken before on some topographic map.
Take times and calculate distance actually traveled by measuring map.
Use this downhill rate for your best overall rate on a rogaine course.

If you've done a rogaine before, see if this rate matches your actual performance in that event. Make up the table as shown above using 66% of your overall rate for the UP/NIGHT legs, and 146% of that rate for the LEVEL rate.

WATER AND ENDURANCE FACTOR: Research shows that your stomach can empty about 800 ml. or 26.4 oz. of water per hour. This varies a little by body weight -- these figures are for a 150-pound person. Your body needs and continually consumes water at about that rate while rogaining. You must find a way to ensure that your team will keep this critical fuel in your system. What do you suppose will happen if you keep moving but don't drink for as long as two hours? Only one hour? And what will happen if SOMEONE on your team fails to drink on schedule?

Actual results for other non-drinkers have been: Leg cramps, severe headaches, fainting, severe dehydration, early retirement, a Search and Rescue call, and a hospital trip. These have all happened in past O' meets and rogaines. You had better set up a drinking schedule and find a way to stick to it. Remember that the feeling of thirst is an emergency signal that comes much too late to be of help. THINK TO DRINK - YOU MUST DRINK TO THINK!
Good luck in your future long-distance navigation events!

I know the title might be the opening line of a Harvey Friedman story, but actually I just wanted to share with you two relatively new products that really work in an orienteering setting. (Info comes from Dr. Forgey, who wrote Wilderness Medicine and spoke at REI recently.)

"DEET Plus" to keep those bugs away. It is as effective as 100% DEET, but with only 17% DEET, bound with a polymer to enhance the effectiveness, the skin absorption issue is much reduced. (About 15% of DEET applied is absorbed through the skin; some have noticed sensitivity.) DEET Plus also contains a 2% Black Fly repellent, which is needed in products where the DEET level is less than 30%. The product sell for under $4 at Sportsmart locally. (I've used it and like it.)

"CopperTone Sport Ultra Sweatproof" is a breathable waterproof suntan lotion. The word "Sport" in the title is key for this Gortex-like protection process. You don't sweat it off, but, unlike with Bullfrog lotion, your skin can breathe, thus you don't blow micro-holes through your protective layer. I've got Linda using it; her only negative comment that it takes a good scrubbing with a loofah to get it off--what more could an orienteer want. You can get an 8 oz bottle at Safeway for about $8.

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and the new Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need? The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for rent at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair of socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Very brief explanation: Using a topographic map, teams (generally pairs) strive to reach as many mapped control points as possible with a 12 or 24 time limit.

Location: The site lies on the east slope of the Cascade Mountains, just east of the of the William O Douglas Wilderness Area, about 30 miles east of the summit of Mt. Rainier on Hwy 410. Allow three hours to reach the site from Seattle. (Detailed driving instruction and motel/camping options mailed with entry form acknowledgment.)
Other 'O-Vents': The Rogaine is the grand finale of a fantastic nine-day orienteering offering by SAMM, CROC, and EOC. Call the hotline at (206) 783-3866 and then select option 4 for more details.

Participation Logistics: Your team will receive a USGS-type topographic map showing the location of all the controls when you check in beginning at about 9 am Saturday. You will be asked to plan your most likely route and leave a copy at registration. A mass start occurs at noon. Depending on the option you choose, you will have 12 or 24 hours to reach as many of the controls as you can. (The more distant and difficult control locations will be worth more points and have a higher control ID to indicate such.) As each control flag you must punch your control card and also sign an "intention sheet" as to where you plan to go next. Regardless, make sure to return to the base camp within your time limit, severe penalties are given for returning late.

Food: By about 6 pm, food service will be set up and available at the "Hash House" located at base camp. The smart teams loop back to rest, refresh, and stoke up for the remainder of the event. Food service continues until the event ends with an Award Ceremony at 1 pm on Sunday. Our backcountry chef, Kent Verbeck, has outlined a sumptuous feast, including fresh Yakima Valley fruits, pastas, and a barbecue!

More Details: Highly competitive teams may reach all the controls. But those out to enjoy some of the great scenery and some challenging navigation will "win" no matter how many controls they find. Many teams volunteer to do a one-control pick-up after the events--this add to the fun since you might drive or hike to a portion of the course you didn't visit before.

Registration: Pre-registration is required. The deadline is August 1st, unless the 50 team maximum is reached earlier, so register early! Send you entry form to: CROC c/o Landchild, 3802 NE 112nd Ave, Vancouver, WA 98682. Fees are $60 per two-person team, and $30 for each additional team member. (Lots of food and other support services, user fees, and organizational costs are covered by the team registration fee.)

Course Options: Your team may register for either the 24 hour "classic" or the 12 hour "recreational" course option. The former will run from noon Saturday to noon Sunday. The latter can be any 12 hours within the 24 hour noon Saturday to noon Sunday period--your team can "clock out" and "in" at the Hash House as often as you like.

Registration Categories: On each course of the two courses, your team may register in any one of the following seven categories: Men, Women, Mixed, and Masters( where all members, whether Men, Women, or Mixed, are over 40) or open-group (any team with more than two participants). The organizer reserves the right to combine categories.

What to Bring?: A compatible partner is most important. Teams are required to stay together (within voice distance). You also must have an ability to navigate with a map and compass. You must carry a whistle, compass, watch, day pack with emergency gear, water (at least two canteens per person), trail food, sunglasses, sunblock, flashlight, trowel and tp... Do not bring other maps nor aerial photographs to use on the course, nor altimeters, GPS devices, etc. (All travel while on the course must be by foot.) We recommend that you leave your pets at home. Please note that our base camp, while nice for camping, does not have water (i.e., it is a dry camp). Thus, everyone should bring their own water for the weekend. Lastly, you are welcome to pitch your tent at base camp anytime after noon on Friday, but please remember that food service doesn't start until Saturday afternoon.

Practice Course: A "trim" or practice course is located near White Pass on Hwy 12. Send $5 for one or $8 for two maps to the above listed registration address. (This area was recently featured in Orienteering North America articles.)

Alan Philip, who many of us have had the pleasure of knowing for years, is the meet director for the BC Champs this year on the newly expanded and full color Ruckle Park map. He sent down a flyer via email, that is a little worse for the wear, but rest assure that the meet will be top notch. Ask anyone who has been to the island before; it is a great O experience, with the new map sure to make it even moreso... --rjh

Meet Director Steve Chatwin, 604 598-5951, and Course Planner Alan Philip would like to extend a warm invitation to all of us from Washington State to join them at the BC Champs this year. This will be a two day total time meet set to COF standards. Whistles are mandatory, and legs should be covered. New Map The 1995 Ruckle Park map is 1:10,000 scale with 5 metre contours. This is a brand new, IOF standard, 5-colour map, just completed by Carl Coger and drawn by Alan Philip. The new map covers an area over twice that of the old Ruckle Park map.

Registration entries postmarked prior to August 23, 1995 will receive maps with courses pre- drawn. Registration after this date will be done at the meet, and the drawing of courses will be included in the competitor's time.

Daily meet fees are: $12 for members and $13.50 for non-members (except on course one and two where fees are $8 and $9.50 respectively) and for juniors the fee is $5 for members and $6.50 for non-members. (If special start times are required, the Registrar must be notified prior to the pre-registration deadline of August 30, 1995.)

Registrations opens at noon on Saturday with the first starts at 1:00 pm. On Sunday it is 9:00 am and 10:00 am, respectively. (Meet Packages will be available at the meet site on Saturday morning starting about 10:00 am.)
There will be a string course set each day. There is no fee for these courses. Child minding services will be provided, by donation, for those who notify the Meet Director by August 30,1995.

We have reserved a large group campsite at Ruckle Park. The campsite is a 100 meter walk-in site on the ocean. Very limited space is available in the parking lot for RV campers on a first come basis. Camping fees are $5.00 / night per tent. There are a large number of Bed and Breakfasts available on Saltspring Island, both in Fulford and Ganges.

Ruckle Provincial Park is located in the south east corner of Saltspring Island. From Swartz Bay on Vancouver Island, take the B.C. ferry to Fulford Harbour (ferry every 90 minutes), turn right on Beaver Point Road for 8 km. From Tsawassen , take either the Swartz Bay ferry and transfer to Saltspring or take the direct ferry to Long Harbour on Saltspring Island. Reservations are required for cars on the Long Harbour ferry. Ferry schedule and reservations - Ph: 386-3431.

A banquet with choice of barbecued chicken or vegetarian, salads, and dessert is planned to take place at the nearby Beaver Point Community Hall (on the map). The banquet will run from 6:00 pm to 9:00 pm. Fees for the banquet must be pre- paid and included with your registration. Please indicate if you desire a vegetarian meal.

The Eastern Washington Orienteering Club and Sacajawea Orienteers present The Fifth Inland Northwest Orienteering Cup B Meet September 16-17, 1995 Riverside State Park, Spokane

Terrain Open, runnable pine forest Map Produced in 1989 for the Washington Centennial Games; 1:15,000 scale 5-color International Orienteering Federation-standard map. Field-checked by Al Smith and Ulf Köster, Cartography by Gary McCausland. Courses Five courses each day, designed by Elis Eberlein. Courses 4 and 5 will follow the model outlined in ONA (May 1995); there will be "classic" (long) courses on Saturday and "sprints" (short) on Sunday. Categories The categories offered are listed on the back of this page. However, some categories may be combined if fewer than five people enter them. Awards All participants (regardless of residence) are eligible for Inland Northwest Cup ribbons to be presented to the top three finishers in each category. Inland Northwest Cup trophies will be awarded to the top woman on Course 4 and the top man on Course 5.

Start Times Saturday - 1:00 to 3:00 P.M. Park at ORV area. Sunday - 9:00 to 11:00 A.M. Park at Bowl and Pitcher. Fees $4 per day for Courses 1 and 2; $6 per day for Courses 3-5. Please make checks payable to EWOC in U.S. funds.
